speaker: interviewer
text: Goszer. Tape number four. Mr. Goszer, you were telling us about your first job in Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was lucky. After three days in Winnipeg, I was approached to work in a camera store. And I probably know as much in a camera store as you know it. And without a language. So I accepted it. And as I walked into the store, my first day working, as a customer came in, I was like a salesman, as a customer came in, I looked in his eyes, and if he pointed, his eyes looked at the film, I knew that he wants a film. And the film was marked the price. And I punched it up on the cash register. And I started to learn the language, and the selling point. Mind you, I used to go to St. John's Tech, I took evening classes for long, long time to help me to know the language. And then I was waiting for the first pay. So this company used to pay twice a month. And I started in 1949. The first two weeks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they gave me an envelope and in the envelope was a check, $100. So I thought it's a lot of the money, you know, at that time. Because I met a lot of fellows, for they came over here. And they used to work in factories. And they used to work overtime in piecework. And if they earned $30 a month, $30 a week was a lot of money. And here I get for two weeks, $100, $50 a week. And naturally, when I left Italy, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brought with me nice suits, nice clothes. Everything nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to go every day white shirt and a tie to work. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I worked for that company.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I met Barbara. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember this like yesterday. In 1949, it was in July, was such a hot day, that when I came from work, I went to a swimming pool. In that time, I used to live on Selkirk Avenue. And the swimming pool was Aikins and Charles, public swimming pool.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there. And I met a fellow, and we were sitting on the shady side, and we were talking. And across on the other side of the pool, there were two girls sitting. And-- and they probably-- the girls saw us, too. So they came over on our side, and they were sitting beside us. So I looked at the girl, and I wanted to say something in Polish to my friend. So as I started to say something, she said, watch out. I understand Polish. So right away, for where do you come? What do you do? Things like this. And I had a camera with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, let me take a picture of you. She says, go ahead. So I took a picture of her. And then I say, tell me your phone number or your address, and I will deliver the picture to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she gave me her phone number and her address. And I made a picture, and I delivered it to her. And that's the way we met.

speaker: interviewee
text: And years ago, in the late '40's and the '50's, there was all the time New Year's Day, Yom Kippur day, that boys used to take out girls and buy them flowers and corsages. And the flood came in Winnipeg in 1950. And she left to Toronto. I did not write to her. She did not write to me. She got another boyfriend. I got another girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: It went by, two years-- almost two years. So I met a friend of Barbara's. And she says, do you write to her? Do you-- I say no.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, send her a greeting card. So I send her a greeting card. So she sends me a letter. So I send her a letter. And we started to write to each other. So I wrote her, if she thinks about me, you must think about Winnipeg. Because I have a good job, and I kept my family here. And I cannot just pick myself up and leave. She came to Winnipeg and we got married in 1952. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's going to be 45 years in 1997 that we are married. And my daughter was born, and then my son was born. And then we had, I had another daughter-- that she died. And the children started to grow up. I earned a good living, but I did not have any money. And we decided to send our children to parochial school, because I believed all the time in education. Education will take you everywhere to make a good living. And if you leave them money, they'll spend it and waste it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started to send them to private school. Sharon finished high school, Joseph Wolinsky Collegiate, with a gold medal. Elliott is a boy-- he liked football and hockey. So he was an average student, but both of them finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sharon, when she finished Joseph Wolinsky Collegiate, she had a grant to join the University of Winnipeg. And she went to University of Winnipeg. And she took-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she started to take psychology.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took, actually, four years at University of Winnipeg. And then she went to University of Manitoba. And she went through university, that it didn't cost me one penny. Only on scholarships.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she got her Master's. Than she went for her PhD. And then she got married in between, while she was at university, but she continued to study. And she got married. And she is happily married, up to now. She has one little boy, Joseph. Elliott was a boy-- like I said-- girls, football, hockey. For him, I had to pay for all the education. But we gave him the choice, whatever they want to be. So he decided that he wants to go into law.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to finish law, got married, has three children. And is happily married up to now. He has three children. Can you tell us a little bit about your early days in Winnipeg? Was it-- how was it-- what was it like when you came to Winnipeg? Did people know anything about your story? Did you talk to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to talk. I used to talk. My wife never talked about it. But I used to talk. But people didn't know it. People didn't believe it. And I don't blame the people, because ourselves, going through the-- such a fire, we couldn't believe ourselves that such a thing existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I couldn't believe that millions of people died. In my hometown, thousands of people died-- and I survived. Now I don't know what to call it. Is it a miracle, is it luck? Is it what? I don't know. Is there a message, Mr. Goszer, you have for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My message to-- to the young people-- fight racism, fight [CRYING] to be a free person, support Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because without your own country, you're a refugee living everywhere. And probably, thanks Israel, that we are proud. Doesn't matter where we live. Because we have all the time to follow on something. Now sure, Canada is a very good country to live. Sure, there is a lot of discrimination, there is a lot of hatred. You see, still, on the street, a lot of murders, that they killed in the war time. But nothing you can do. But that's the minority. But we live in the majority. And the majority of people are good people. And we have the freedom to do, to go whatever we want to. So we have to keep up and support a good democracy-- very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Goszer, thank you very much for sharing your experiences with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Goszer, can you tell us about the people in this photograph? Who they are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. On the extreme left is my mother. And her name is Pesia. On my extreme right is my father, that his name is Eliahu. In the middle first row, myself. Behind me is my brother, Boris. And below my father is my sister, Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, perhaps, when it was taken, and what was the occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been taken at least 65 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the occasion maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: To send pictures to the United States. That was the occasion. Let's get a professional photographer-- and I even remember the photographer's name, it was Signor. He had a studio, and we came, and he took that picture. And that picture was sent to the United States. And when we came to Canada, my mother's cousin send the picture to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us, Mr. Goszer, about the children in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself and my sister, Rachel. I'm here, 10 years old, and my sister is about six years old. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to go to professional photographers, take pictures, and send them to the relatives in the United States, or in other cities. I can remember that shirt that I wear. As you see it, it's a black, satin shirt, and it has embroidery all the way-- all the way. And I remember this picture when it was taken. It's probably about 65 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell us, Mr. Goszer, about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, and her name is Hache. She was married, and had two children and her husband. And I remember seeing when the Ukrainian police cut her, took her out from her house, and took away her and the two children to be shot. And that's where she was shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this young man, Mr. Goszer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture taken in 1944 when I came from the partisans to my home town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there about 23, 24.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell us about this picture, Mr. Goszer?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture as you see on the extreme left and on the right is a friend of mine. We were, at their time, in the officer school for three months. And we were good buddies. So we took a picture together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where? Do you recall where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken somewhere [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] around Lublin, but I'm not too sure the name of the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell us please, Mr. Goszer, about the circumstances of this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This picture was taken in Italy in 1945. On the extreme left is my mother. And on the right is my sister. On the left, Emma, myself, and on the right is my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell us about the place and the people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1948 in a DP camp called Trani. On the extreme left is my sister. In the middle is my mother. And on the extreme right is my brother, Boris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this photo ID and when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken 1947. The reason for it was taken, because I came to Canada privately. And I had to have a passport. So we went to the Polish consulate, and he did find out all about us, and he gave us Polish passports to emigrate anywhere we want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on this passport, I have my Canadian visa. And I have a French visa, and I have a Polish visa. And I think it was given to us five years that any time we can return to these countries. What can you tell us Mr. Goszer about this young gentleman?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1947. And the reason I went to a professional photographer to take it because all my friends wanted pictures. They were leaving to Israel, to the United States. They were living all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they insisted I should take a picture, sign it, and give it to them. And that's what I did. And who is this couple in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is, on the extreme left, is my wife, Barbara. And as you see, it's me on the right. And we used to own this home. We used to live on 450 Royal Avenue. And there must have been an occasion, because we were dressed up. Usually we used to bum around in jeans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in 1973.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what you were doing and where you were in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken on 396 Main Street. That's where I had my store. And the store was called Adam's Camera Shop Limited. And we had a promotion on Pentax cameras. That's why you see me in that green uniform. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were very successful with this promotion. And I used to-- very often, we used to make from different manufacturers, I used to have demonstrations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this picture, I selected to be photographed because my son-in-law, Steven Tritt, couldn't make it today. So I thought to have him with us as a family, to have a record. So it's Steven Tritt, my son-in-law, Sharon Goszer-Tritt, my daughter, and my son, Elliott Goszer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Steven is to the extreme left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Steven is on the extreme left, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us who these children are in the photographs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my two grandsons. My son's children. On the extreme right is Sam. And on the extreme left is Nicholas. Sam is in grade two grammar school. And Nicholas is in grade one in grammar school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this little person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is the princess. Her real name is Sarah. But I call her princess because she's the only one girl as a granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's a year and a half-- not quite.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this young person in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Joseph Tritt, my grandson. He belongs to Sharon and Steven Tritt. And as a matter of fact, this picture, we just got the other day. It's a school picture. He goes to grammar school. And he is in grade one. And he has a very good friend. He has a dog. And the dog's name is Walnut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Goszer, please introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my extreme right is my daughter, Paula. And the princess is Sarah. But I call her princess because she is the only one girl of three boys. Next to Princess is standing my son, Elliott. And next to my son, is standing my daughter Sharon. And actually myself and my wife. And we are responsible for them to be here today. But without us they wouldn't be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does anyone, a family member, went to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure my sister wouldn't let an opportunity to say something get away, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I always let you go first.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Dad, we'll talk to you. We'll tell you we're proud of you for having the courage to go through with this today, and talk about your experiences, and what you went through during the war years and the Holocaust. And we know you didn't talk about it very much as we were growing up, but we know you thought about it a lot, and you read about it. And even though we know you went through a lot during the war, despite that, you were able to inject our lives with fun and humor, and support us in the things that we did in our lives. And I know Steven and Joseph are disappointed they couldn't come today, but we all love you, and we're glad that you decided to make this contribution to history.

speaker: interviewee
text: With or without your ponytail.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why we photographed pictures that Steven is on the picture. And the children with three boys-- Sam, Nicholas, and Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess what I would add to that is that, for me, personally, I think our father gave us the importance of Jewish identity, and as well, he always had this unique ability to be content with wherever he was in life. That's something that I personally always admired in you, Dad. And I think I can say for all of us, we love you, and we're proud of you, and that's what I've got to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
